summary: Ilya Sutskever discusses the transition from the 'age of scaling' back to the 'age of research,' highlighting that current AI models generalize dramatically worse than humans. He explores the concept of human emotions acting as an evolutionary value function that enables robust, unsupervised learning. Finally, he argues that AGI should be conceptualized not as an omniscient finished product, but as a highly capable learner—akin to a superintelligent 15-year-old—that relies on continual learning. Ilya Sutskever discusses the transition from pre-training to continual learning, arguing that AGI will resemble a highly efficient learner rather than a finished omniscient mind. He advocates for incremental deployment to help society grasp AI's power, aligning AI to care for sentient life, and relying on top-down aesthetic beliefs to guide AI research.

principles.statement: We are returning to the age of research because scaling alone is no longer enough to transform models.
principles.rationale: While scaling pre-training was a reliable recipe, data is finite. Simply multiplying compute by 100x will not transform everything; researchers must find fundamentally new and more productive ways to use massive compute.
principles.source_quote: But now that compute is big, compute is now very big, in some sense we are back to the age of research.

principles.statement: The most fundamental flaw in current models is their inability to generalize as well as humans.
principles.rationale: Humans can learn robustly from very few samples and without explicit, verifiable rewards, whereas models require massive amounts of data and bespoke RL environments to achieve similar competence.
principles.source_quote: The thing which I think is the most fundamental is that these models somehow just generalize dramatically worse than people. It's super obvious.

principles.statement: Superintelligence should be deployed as a highly capable learner, not a finished mind.
principles.rationale: Just as humans rely on continual learning rather than being born with all knowledge, an AGI will likely start as a fast learner that masters specific jobs through trial and error in the real world.
principles.source_quote: I produce a superintelligent 15-year-old that's very eager to go. They don't know very much at all, a great student, very eager. You go and be a programmer, you go and be a doctor, go and learn.

principles.statement: Value functions improve RL efficiency, but they are not strictly necessary for capabilities.
principles.rationale: A value function allows a model to short-circuit bad paths early, saving compute and time, but the same end result can theoretically be achieved without it, just at a slower pace.
principles.source_quote: I think anything you can do with a value function, you can do without, just more slowly.

principles.statement: Deploy AI incrementally and in advance so people can feel its power.
principles.rationale: AGI is difficult to imagine because it doesn't exist yet. People and companies will only change their behaviors and take safety seriously once they actually see and feel the power of the systems.
principles.source_quote: You’ve got to be showing the thing. I maintain that most people who work on AI also can't imagine it because it's too different from what people see on a day-to-day basis.

principles.statement: Align AI to care about sentient life, not just human life.
principles.rationale: In the future, the vast majority of sentient beings will be AIs. It may also be easier to build an AI that cares about sentient life because the AI itself will be sentient, leveraging emergent properties similar to human empathy.
principles.source_quote: It's the AI that's robustly aligned to care about sentient life specifically. I think in particular, there's a case to be made that it will be easier to build an AI that cares about sentient life than an AI that cares about human life alone, because the AI itself will be sentient.

principles.statement: Research must be guided by a top-down belief rooted in beauty, simplicity, and correct inspiration from the brain.
principles.rationale: When experiments fail or contradict your hypothesis, you need a strong theoretical or aesthetic conviction to know whether to keep debugging the code or to abandon the direction entirely.
principles.source_quote: The top-down belief is the thing that sustains you when the experiments contradict you. Because if you trust the data all the time, well sometimes you can be doing the correct thing but there's a bug.

frameworks.name: Top-Down Research Taste
frameworks.when_to_apply: When deciding which AI research directions to pursue and whether to persist through experimental failures.
steps: Look for correct inspiration from the brain (e.g., neurons, distributed representation, learning from experience).
Ensure the idea possesses beauty, simplicity, and elegance, leaving no room for ugliness.
Form a strong top-down belief based on these aesthetics.
Use this top-down belief to sustain your effort and keep debugging when initial experiments contradict you.
frameworks.source_quote: It's beauty, simplicity, elegance, correct inspiration from the brain. All of those things need to be present at the same time. The more they are present, the more confident you can be in a top-down belief.

mental_models.name: The Two Students Analogy
mental_models.description: A metaphor to explain the difference between current AI training and true generalization. One student memorizes 10,000 hours of specific problems (like current models trained on massive data), while another has the 'it' factor and generalizes well after only 100 hours of practice.
mental_models.example: Used to explain why models can be superhuman at competitive programming evals but fail to exercise tasteful judgment or fix simple bugs in real-world codebases.

mental_models.name: Emotions as a Value Function
mental_models.description: Viewing human emotions as an evolutionary, hardcoded value function that modulates learning and decision-making, allowing humans to self-correct and learn efficiently without external teachers.
mental_models.example: A teenager learning to drive self-corrects quickly because they have an internal sense (value function) of how badly or unconfidently they are driving, enabling rapid unsupervised learning.

mental_models.name: The Superintelligent 15-Year-Old
mental_models.description: A lens for conceptualizing AGI deployment. Instead of a system that already knows how to do every job in the economy, AGI is a system with a foundation of skills and an extreme eagerness and capacity to learn on the job.
mental_models.example: Deploying the AI into an organization where it undergoes a trial-and-error period to learn how to be a programmer or a doctor, mimicking human continual learning.

mental_models.name: Superintelligence as a Continual Learner
mental_models.description: Viewing AGI not as a finished mind that already knows every job, but as a highly efficient learning algorithm that starts with a foundation of skills and learns on the job through trial and error.
mental_models.example: Deploying a superintelligent model that acts like an eager 15-year-old student who goes into the economy to learn how to be a programmer or a doctor.

mental_models.name: Evolution as an Alignment Existence Proof
mental_models.description: Using human evolution as evidence that high-level, complex desires (like social standing) can be reliably hard-coded into an intelligent system by a non-intelligent optimization process (the genome).
mental_models.example: Pointing out that evolution successfully endowed humans with deep social desires, even though there is no simple low-level 'sensor' for social standing like there is for smell.

heuristics: If humans exhibit great learning ability in a domain that didn't exist until recently (like coding), it indicates humans simply have better fundamental machine learning, not just an evolutionary prior.
Anything you can do with a value function, you can do without, just more slowly.
Ugliness, there's no room for ugliness.
You’ve got to be showing the thing.

signature_quotes.text: I think the models seem smarter than their economic impact would imply.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the disconnect between high performance on AI evaluations and the actual economic utility felt in the real world.

signature_quotes.text: This is an example of how language affects thought. 'Scaling' is just one word, but it's such a powerful word because it informs people what to do.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how the term 'scaling' drove the entire AI industry's strategy from 2020 to 2025.

signature_quotes.text: But now that compute is big, compute is now very big, in some sense we are back to the age of research.
signature_quotes.context: Arguing that simply scaling up existing recipes is no longer enough to guarantee transformative results.

signature_quotes.text: The thing which I think is the most fundamental is that these models somehow just generalize dramatically worse than people. It's super obvious.
signature_quotes.context: Identifying the core crux of what is missing in current deep learning systems.

signature_quotes.text: If you think about the term 'AGI', especially in the context of pre-training, you will realize that a human being is not an AGI.
signature_quotes.context: Discussing how pre-training overshot the target and how humans rely on continual learning instead of knowing everything upfront.

signature_quotes.text: I produce a superintelligent 15-year-old that's very eager to go. They don't know very much at all, a great student, very eager. You go and be a programmer, you go and be a doctor, go and learn.
signature_quotes.context: Explaining his vision of how superintelligence will be deployed as a continual learning process.

signature_quotes.text: The whole problem is the power. When the power is really big, what's going to happen?
signature_quotes.context: Explaining why AGI is difficult to imagine and why its future capabilities are the core concern.

signature_quotes.text: The top-down belief is the thing that sustains you when the experiments contradict you.
signature_quotes.context: Describing his research taste and how to persist through bugs and failures.

signature_quotes.text: In theory, there is no difference between theory and practice. In practice, there is.
signature_quotes.context: Pushing back against the theoretical argument that a single company will easily dominate every niche in the economy.

anti_patterns.description: Human researchers inadvertently hacking rewards by designing RL environments specifically to perform well on public evals.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It creates a disconnect between eval performance and actual real-world performance, leading to models that look great on paper but fail at basic generalization tasks.

anti_patterns.description: Believing that simply scaling up the current recipe by 100x will transform everything.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Pre-training data is finite, and current models suffer from fundamental generalization issues that scale alone cannot fix.

anti_patterns.description: Defining AGI as a finished mind that can immediately do every single job a human can do.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: It overshoots the target and ignores the reality of intelligence, which relies heavily on continual learning and trial-and-error in the real world.

anti_patterns.description: Assuming AGI means a finished system that already knows how to do every job in the economy.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Humans aren't born knowing everything; they rely on continual learning. AGI will likely be a highly efficient learning algorithm that learns on the job, rather than a pre-packaged omniscient mind.

anti_patterns.description: Trusting the data and experiments all the time without a top-down belief.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: If you only trust the data, a simple bug in your code might convince you to abandon a correct and fundamental research direction because you don't know the bug is there.

anti_patterns.description: Relying solely on pre-training to generate diversity among AI agents.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Pre-trained models end up being pretty much the same because they are trained on the same data. Meaningful diversity requires competition or post-training RL where agents are incentivized to differentiate.
